event: Austronesian-speaking peoples, ancestors of Taiwan's indigenous tribes, settle the island, making it a likely homeland of the wider Austronesian expansion.
The Neolithic Dabenkeng (Tapenkeng) culture flourishes with corded-ware pottery and early agriculture along Taiwan's coasts.
Chinese records from the Three Kingdoms state of Wu describe an expedition to an island often identified as Taiwan.
Yuan-dynasty traveler Wang Dayuan describes Taiwan and the Penghu Islands in his account 'Daoyi Zhilue'.
Portuguese sailors sight the island and name it 'Ilha Formosa' (Beautiful Island), a name long used by Europeans.
The Dutch East India Company establishes Fort Zeelandia at Tayouan, beginning Dutch colonial rule over southwestern Taiwan.
Spain establishes settlements in northern Taiwan, founding fortifications at Keelung and later Tamsui.
The Dutch expel the Spanish from northern Taiwan, consolidating European control of the island under the VOC.
Chinese settlers stage the Guo Huaiyi Rebellion against Dutch rule, which is suppressed with heavy loss of life.
Ming loyalist Koxinga (Zheng Chenggong) drives out the Dutch and founds the Kingdom of Tungning on Taiwan.
The Qing dynasty defeats the Zheng regime at the Battle of Penghu and annexes Taiwan into the Chinese empire.
Qing authorities incorporate Taiwan as a prefecture of Fujian Province, opening it to regulated Han Chinese migration.
Zhu Yigui leads a large anti-Qing rebellion, briefly capturing much of the island before being defeated.
The Lin Shuangwen uprising, the largest Qing-era rebellion in Taiwan, erupts and is crushed after more than a year.
Following the Second Opium War, treaty ports including Tamsui and Anping open Taiwan to expanded foreign trade.
The Mudan Incident, in which Paiwan people kill shipwrecked Ryukyuan sailors, sparks a diplomatic crisis with Japan.
Japan launches a punitive military expedition to southern Taiwan, exposing the limits of Qing control over indigenous areas.
The Qing elevate Taiwan to a full province, with reformer Liu Mingchuan as its first governor pursuing modernization.
The Treaty of Shimonoseki cedes Taiwan to Japan; the short-lived Republic of Formosa resists before Japanese forces take control.
Japan completes the north-south Western Trunk railway, integrating Taiwan's economy under colonial development.
The Tapani Incident, a major Han-led armed uprising against Japanese rule, is brutally suppressed.
The Wushe (Musha) Incident sees Seediq people rise against Japanese rule, prompting a harsh military reprisal.
Japan stages the Taiwan Exhibition in Taipei, showcasing four decades of colonial modernization.
Japan surrenders at the end of World War II and the Republic of China takes administrative control of Taiwan.
The February 28 Incident erupts as anti-government protests are violently suppressed, killing thousands and beginning the White Terror.
After losing the Chinese Civil War, Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government retreats to Taiwan and imposes martial law.
With the Korean War underway, the United States deploys the Seventh Fleet to the Taiwan Strait, shielding the island.
The First Taiwan Strait Crisis prompts the Sino-American Mutual Defense Treaty between the ROC and the United States.
The Second Taiwan Strait Crisis sees intense PRC shelling of the offshore island of Kinmen.
US economic aid to Taiwan ends as the island's export-oriented industrialization takes off, beginning the 'Taiwan Miracle'.
The United Nations seats the People's Republic of China and the ROC loses its UN membership and Security Council seat.
Chiang Kai-shek dies, ending nearly three decades as the dominant ruler of Nationalist Taiwan.
The United States switches diplomatic recognition to Beijing; the Kaohsiung Incident galvanizes Taiwan's democracy movement.
The Democratic Progressive Party is founded, the first opposition party formed in defiance of one-party rule.
President Chiang Ching-kuo lifts martial law after 38 years, beginning Taiwan's democratic liberalization.
Lee Teng-hui becomes president, the first native-born Taiwanese to lead the ROC, accelerating democratization.
Taiwan ends the 'Period of Mobilization for Suppression of Communist Rebellion', formally easing its war footing with the mainland.
Taiwan holds its first direct presidential election, re-electing Lee Teng-hui amid Chinese missile tests in the strait.
Chen Shui-bian wins the presidency, achieving the first peaceful transfer of power away from the Kuomintang.
Taiwan joins the World Trade Organization under the name 'Chinese Taipei'.
Taipei 101, then the world's tallest building, opens as a symbol of Taiwan's economic and technological prowess.
Ma Ying-jeou wins the presidency, returning the Kuomintang to power and easing cross-strait economic ties.
The Sunflower Movement occupies the legislature in protest against a cross-strait services trade pact with China.
Tsai Ing-wen is elected Taiwan's first female president as the DPP wins control of the legislature.
Taiwan becomes the first jurisdiction in Asia to legalize same-sex marriage.
Taiwan wins international praise for its early and effective response to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Lai Ching-te of the DPP is elected president, marking a third consecutive term for the party.
